examples.title: IaaS - Virtual Machine Management Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) by simulating virtual machine lifecycle management - the user manages OS, storage, and networking
examples.code:
# IaaS Example: Virtual Machine Management
# In IaaS, user manages: OS, runtime, data, applications
# Provider manages: physical servers, virtualization, networking, storage

import time
from datetime import datetime

class IaaSVirtualMachine:
    def __init__(self, vm_id, cpu_cores, ram_gb, disk_gb, os_type):
        self.vm_id = vm_id
        self.cpu_cores = cpu_cores
        self.ram_gb = ram_gb
        self.disk_gb = disk_gb
        self.os_type = os_type
        self.status = "stopped"
        self.created_at = datetime.now()
    
    def start(self):
        if self.status == "stopped":
            self.status = "running"
            print(f"[{self.vm_id}] VM started - OS booted: {self.os_type}")
            return True
        return False
    
    def stop(self):
        if self.status == "running":
            self.status = "stopped"
            print(f"[{self.vm_id}] VM stopped")
            return True
        return False
    
    def configure_firewall(self, port, protocol="tcp"):
        print(f"[{self.vm_id}] Firewall configured: {protocol}/{port}")
        print(f"  -> User manages OS-level security")
    
    def deploy_application(self, app_name):
        print(f"[{self.vm_id}] Deploying {app_name} on {self.os_type}")
        print(f"  -> User manages runtime and applications")

# Simulate IaaS VM lifecycle
print("=== IaaS: Virtual Machine Management ===")
vm1 = IaaSVirtualMachine("vm-001", 4, 16, 500, "Ubuntu 22.04 LTS")
vm1.start()
vm1.configure_firewall(8080, "tcp")
vm1.deploy_application("Web Server")
vm1.stop()
print("\nIaaS User Responsibility: OS, middleware, runtime, data, applications")
examples.explanation: This example simulates IaaS where the user provisions and manages virtual machines. The user is responsible for the operating system, runtime environments, applications, and data. The cloud provider manages the underlying physical infrastructure. Key operations include starting/stopping VMs, configuring network security, and deploying applications.

examples.title: PaaS - Application Deployment Platform
examples.description: Demonstrates Platform as a Service (PaaS) by simulating deployment to a managed platform - the user only manages applications and data
examples.code:
# PaaS Example: Application Deployment to Cloud Platform
# In PaaS, user manages: applications, data
# Provider manages: OS, runtime, middleware, runtime, virtualization, storage, networking

import time
from datetime import datetime

class PaaSPlatform:
    def __init__(self, platform_name):
        self.platform_name = platform_name
        self.apps = []
        self.services = []
    
    def create_service(self, service_type, service_name):
        service = {
            "type": service_type,
            "name": service_name,
            "status": "provisioned"
        }
        self.services.append(service)
        print(f"[PaaS] {service_type} service '{service_name}' provisioned")
        print(f"  -> Platform manages {service_type} infrastructure")
        return service
    
    def deploy_application(self, app_name, language, framework):
        deployment = {
            "app_name": app_name,
            "language": language,
            "framework": framework,
            "status": "deploying",
            "deployed_at": datetime.now()
        }
        # Simulate deployment time
        print(f"[PaaS] Deploying {app_name} ({language}/{framework})...")
        time.sleep(0.1)
        deployment["status"] = "running"
        deployment["url"] = f"https://{app_name}.{self.platform_name}.cloud"
        self.apps.append(deployment)
        print(f"[PaaS] Application deployed successfully!")
        print(f"  -> URL: {deployment['url']}")
        print(f"  -> Auto-scaling: enabled")
        print(f"  -> Load balancing: configured")
        return deployment
    
    def scale_application(self, app_name, instances):
        print(f"[PaaS] Scaling {app_name} to {instances} instances")
        print(f"  -> Platform handles load balancing and scaling")
    
    def get_logs(self, app_name):
        print(f"[PaaS] Streaming logs for {app_name}:")
        print(f"  [2024-01-15 10:30:01] Application started")
        print(f"  [2024-01-15 10:30:02] Server listening on port 8000")

# Simulate PaaS deployment
print("=== PaaS: Application Deployment Platform ===")
heroku_like = PaaSPlatform("CloudPlatform")

# Create database service (managed by platform)
db = heroku_like.create_service("PostgreSQL", "user-db")

# Create cache service
cache = heroku_like.create_service("Redis", "session-cache")

# Deploy application (user only manages the code)
app = heroku_like.deploy_application("my-webapp", "Python", "Django")
heroku_like.scale_application("my-webapp", 3)
heroku_like.get_logs("my-webapp")
print("\nPaaS User Responsibility: applications and data only")
examples.explanation: This example demonstrates PaaS where the cloud provider manages the underlying infrastructure including operating systems, runtime environments, and middleware. Users only need to upload their application code, and the platform handles deployment, scaling, load balancing, and infrastructure management. This allows developers to focus solely on application logic.

examples.title: SaaS - Cloud-Based Software Service Integration
examples.description: Demonstrates Software as a Service (SaaS) by integrating with a cloud-hosted application - the user uses the software as-is without managing anything
examples.code:
# SaaS Example: Using a Cloud-Based CRM Application
# In SaaS, user manages: just the usage/access (maybe data they input)
# Provider manages: everything - applications, data, runtime, OS, storage, servers

import json
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Dict, List

class SaaSCustomerRelationshipManager:
    """
    Simulates a SaaS CRM application running entirely in the cloud.
    Users access via web browser/mobile app - no installation required.
    """
    
    def __init__(self, tenant_id):
        self.tenant_id = tenant_id
        self.customers = []
        self.contacts = []
        self.deals = []
        self.api_endpoint = "https://crm.saas-platform.com/api/v2"
    
    def authenticate(self, username, password) -> Dict:
        """Simulate OAuth authentication - provider handles security"""
        print(f"[SaaS] Authenticating user: {username}")
        print(f"  -> Provider manages authentication, SSL, security")
        return {
            "access_token": "eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIs...",
            "token_type": "Bearer",
            "expires_in": 3600
        }
    
    def add_customer(self, customer_data: Dict) -> Dict:
        """Add customer to SaaS CRM - data stored in provider's database"""
        customer = {
            "id": f"cust-{len(self.customers) + 1}",
            **customer_data,
            "created_at": datetime.now().isoformat(),
            "created_by": "user@example.com"
        }
        self.customers.append(customer)
        print(f"[SaaS] Customer added: {customer['name']}")
        print(f"  -> Provider manages: database, backups, disaster recovery")
        return customer
    
    def track_deal(self, deal_data: Dict) -> Dict:
        """Track sales deal - provider handles all data processing"""
        deal = {
            "id": f"deal-{len(self.deals) + 1}",
            **deal_data,
            "stage": "negotiation",
            "last_updated": datetime.now().isoformat()
        }
        self.deals.append(deal)
        print(f"[SaaS] Deal tracked: {deal['value']} - {deal['customer']}")
        print(f"  -> Provider manages: storage, processing, analytics")
        return deal
    
    def generate_report(self, report_type: str) -> Dict:
        """Generate analytics report - provider's servers process data"""
        report = {
            "type": report_type,
            "generated_at": datetime.now().isoformat(),
            "total_customers": len(self.customers),
            "total_deals": len(self.deals),
            "total_value": sum(d.get("value", 0) for d in self.deals),
            "insights": [
                "30% increase in customer retention",
                "Top region: Delhi-NCR",
                "Best performing product: Cloud Services"
            ]
        }
        print(f"[SaaS] Report generated: {report_type}")
        print(f"  -> Provider's analytics engine processed data")
        return report
    
    def get_usage_stats(self) -> Dict:
        """Get usage statistics - provider monitors all usage"""
        return {
            "api_calls_today": 1247,
            "storage_used_gb": 2.3,
            "active_users": 15,
            "uptime_percentage": 99.98
        }

# Simulate SaaS CRM usage
print("=== SaaS: Cloud-Based CRM Application ===")
crm = SaaSCustomerRelationshipManager("org-12345")

# Authenticate (provider handles security)
token = crm.authenticate("user@example.com", "password")

# Add customer (provider manages database)
customer = crm.add_customer({
    "name": "Tech Innovations Pvt Ltd",
    "email": "contact@techinnovations.com",
    "industry": "Technology",
    "region": "Delhi-NCR"
})

# Track deals (provider handles data processing)
deal1 = crm.track_deal({
    "customer": "Tech Innovations Pvt Ltd",
    "product": "Enterprise Cloud Suite",
    "value": 500000
})

# Generate report (provider's analytics)
report = crm.generate_report("sales-performance")
print(f"\nReport: {json.dumps(report, indent=2)}")

# Check usage stats
stats = crm.get_usage_stats()
print(f"Usage Stats: {json.dumps(stats, indent=2)}")

print("\nSaaS User Responsibility: Using the application (minimal)")
print("Provider manages: EVERYTHING else")
examples.explanation: This example demonstrates SaaS where a complete software application is hosted in the cloud and made available to users over the internet. Users access the software through a web browser or mobile app without any installation. The cloud provider manages everything including the application, data, runtime, operating system, storage, and physical infrastructure. Users simply use the software and perhaps manage their own user data.
